Framework · free to use

B2B content marketing playbook for the post-AI-Overview era.

The B2B content playbook of 2022 — write helpful, optimize for keywords, hope for SEO traffic — is broken in 2026. AI Overviews have collapsed mid-funnel traffic; generic content is being penalized; the distinctive operator-led content is winning. Here's what to actually do.

By Bill Colbert · Founder, Treetop Growth Strategy
Published May 2026 · More from the library
The shift

What changed in 18 months

Generic informational queries are now answered directly by AI Overviews and chat assistants — those clicks no longer come to your site. What does come: queries where the user wants a specific opinion, a specific framework, a specific operator's take.

Implication: stop writing helpful generic content. Start writing distinctive operator-led content. The traffic shape changes — less volume, higher intent — and the buyer is more qualified.

The new content stack

What to actually publish

1. Opinionated framework content

How-tos, frameworks, and methodologies with a specific point of view. Not "5 tips for X" but "Why X is the wrong question to ask, and what to ask instead." Becomes citable by AI assistants AND by other writers.

2. Operator-led narrative

Founder/exec/SME bylined posts grounded in specific recent experience. "Here is what we learned shipping X this quarter." Drives the trust that converts.

3. Customer stories with real numbers

Specific outcomes with specific situations. Composite case studies (clearly labeled) work fine when real names are sensitive.

4. Comparison and decision content

Buyer-stage content: "X vs Y," "when to choose X," "how much does X cost." High intent, high conversion.

5. Internal knowledge made external

If your team has an internal framework, document, or checklist that you use repeatedly — publish it. Distinctive by definition.

What to stop publishing

Cut from the calendar

  • Generic "What is X" articles competing on volume.
  • Listicles without a specific POV.
  • AI-generated content not anchored by a named author.
  • Roundups of other people's quotes without your own analysis.
  • Content written for SEO algorithms rather than for a specific reader.
Production model

Team shape for 2026

  • One editor / strategist who owns voice and POV.
  • One operator-writer who can both report and write (or, alternatively, ghost for execs who can report but cannot write).
  • AI tooling for research, briefs, drafts, and repurposing.
  • One designer shared across content + brand + product marketing.
  • Subject matter experts who contribute interviews, not full articles.

A 3-4 person content function with this shape can outproduce a 7-person 2022-era content team, with better quality, at lower cost.

LLM-O

Optimizing to be cited by AI assistants

  • Specific, sourced claims. AI Overviews cite confident, attributable assertions.
  • Clean URL structure, clear page titles, clean H2s.
  • FAQ blocks with structured Q&A schema.
  • Author bios with credentials. Bylines matter.
  • Recency signals. Old pages get superseded.
  • Distinctive POV. Generic restatements of consensus are not citation-worthy.
Measurement

What to track in the new model

  • Pipeline attribution by content piece. Which specific posts drive demos? Bet more on those.
  • Time on page for high-intent content. If buyers spend 4+ minutes on your comparison pages, you are doing it right.
  • Branded search volume. Your category awareness shows up here before anywhere else.
  • Mentions in AI assistant answers. Hard to measure directly; track citations to your domain in public AI tool outputs.
  • Subscriber growth (email, podcast, etc.). First-party audience is the long-term asset.
